body:
## Implementation Plan Complete — BIN-87 Cloud Backup & Sync

Plan written for encrypted cloud backup with full UI and mocked backend for v1.

### Summary

- **8 new files**: types/sync.ts, lib/sync.ts (Web Crypto API encryption + mock cloud), SyncStatusChip, ConflictPanel, SyncSettings with CSS modules
- **3 modified files**: settings/page.tsx (→ client), story/[id]/page.tsx (chip + conflict panel)
- Encryption is production-ready: PBKDF2 key derivation, AES-GCM 256 — passphrase never stored
- Cloud storage mocked via localStorage `sync-cloud-*` keys with clear TODO for real API swap
- Three-way conflict resolution: keep-local, keep-remote, keep-both
- Restore flow in Settings with snapshot list

See `<plan>` block in description for exact types, CSS, and acceptance mapping.

Assign to: Code Monkey

## UX goal
Deliver a trust-first sync experience that feels invisible during normal writing, but immediately clear when attention is required (conflict/failure). Setup must be optional, privacy-forward, and completable in under 2 minutes.

## Information hierarchy
1. Primary: Writing continuity confidence (current sync status, last successful backup, whether this device is protected).
2. Secondary: Quick actions (Enable sync, Retry, Resolve conflict, Restore snapshot).
3. Tertiary: Technical detail (encryption/passphrase info, device list, timestamp/history).

## Layout and components
- Settings becomes the control center for sync in a new section under existing page structure: "Cloud Backup & Sync".
- In `src/app/settings/page.tsx`, keep the current stacked group pattern and add one full-width card block with:
  - Top row: feature title, short privacy line, primary toggle/button (Enable/Disable).
  - Setup steps: passphrase create/confirm, device link state, final confirmation.
  - Post-setup summary: last backup time, this device state, connected devices count, restore entry point.
- In `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx`, add lightweight status visibility in both editor and corkboard headers:
  - Right side status chip near `ViewModeSwitch` (Syncing, Synced, Conflict, Failed).
  - When conflict/failed: expandable inline alert panel under header with single primary action.
- Restore UX: entry from Settings and per-story context. Use a snapshot list view (timestamp + story title + size/detail) with explicit confirmation before restore.

## Interaction states
- Idle: "Sync off" with clear CTA to enable.
- Loading: inline skeleton/text for setup checks and history load; keep editor usable while syncing in background.
- Empty: no snapshots yet, no linked devices, or no conflicts; each empty state includes one clear next step.
- Error: persistent inline banner/panel with plain-language cause + Retry; never silent failures.
- Success: concise confirmation row with last sync timestamp and subtle visual confirmation.

## Accessibility
- Keyboard: all controls reachable in logical order; setup flow supports full keyboard operation without pointer-only steps.
- Focus: use existing visible focus treatment and ensure conflict/error panels receive programmatic focus when opened.
- Contrast: keep text/background pairs on approved grayscale tokens with strong contrast for status and body copy.
- Text clarity: avoid security jargon; prefer writer-facing language ("backup", "restore draft", "keep both versions").

## Visual style
- Match current grayscale system from `src/styles/tokens.css` only.
- Use `--color-polar-100`/`--color-white` surfaces, `--color-fashion-300` dividers, `--color-black-700` primary text, `--color-cement-400` helper text.
- Sync status should rely on text + icon/shape cues, not color alone.
- Keep the existing clean, low-noise Living Writer-style hierarchy: strong headings, restrained chrome, writing-first density.

## Responsive behavior
- Desktop: settings card uses clear vertical sections; story header keeps status chip and mode switch on one row when space allows.
- Mobile: stack header controls in story view (status row above mode switch), preserve tap targets, and keep conflict actions one-click.
- Setup and restore flows should avoid modal-only dependence on mobile; prefer inline sections/panels that scroll naturally.

## Codebase alignment
- Reviewed existing architecture and styling primitives:
  - `src/app/layout.tsx`
  - `src/components/AppShell/AppShell.tsx`
  - `src/components/Header/Header.tsx`
  - `src/components/ContentContainer/ContentContainer.tsx`
  - `src/app/settings/page.tsx` + `src/app/settings/page.module.css`
  - `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx` + `src/app/story/[id]/page.module.css`
  - `src/styles/tokens.css`
- Brief is aligned to current page-level CSS Modules and tokenized grayscale approach.

## Reuse vs new build
- Reuse:
  - Existing Settings grouped-section pattern and card styling conventions.
  - Existing story header composition and `ViewModeSwitch` placement model.
  - Existing token system (`tokens.css`) and spacing/type scale.
- New build:
  - Sync status chip component pattern.
  - Conflict/failure inline panel pattern.
  - Snapshot restore list row pattern.

## Feasibility notes
- Current app appears local-first and UI-only; sync states will need async state modeling that does not block editor typing.
- Conflict resolution should preserve both versions by default and force an explicit user choice to merge/keep-both before clearing state.
- Encryption setup must include passphrase confirmation and recovery warning text; losing the passphrase should be clearly communicated as non-recoverable content risk.

## Constraints / implementation notes
- Keep changes scoped to current route structure (`/settings`, `/story/[id]`) rather than introducing new top-level nav routes.
- Use one-click actions for critical flows: Enable sync, Retry sync, Keep both, Restore snapshot.
- Keep header height behavior in mind (`src/components/Header/Header.module.css` uses a fixed 64px shell header); additional sync UI should live in page-level headers, not global shell header, unless explicitly approved as structural change.
- Preserve existing responsive breakpoints already used across app CSS modules.

## Handoff
Assign to: Founding Engineer

## Decision
approve

## Differentiation thesis
Cross-device sync alone is table stakes, but this ticket explicitly pushes past parity by combining privacy-first encryption with conflict-safe restore. That gives us a trust-and-reliability position versus competitors that emphasize convenience sync but do not foreground user-held encryption keys and conflict-preserving recovery choices.

## Competitor evidence
- LivingWriter markets automatic cloud sync and backups as core reliability value: https://livingwriter.com/about
- Campfire positions cloud sync and backup behavior (including autosave cadence and common sync caveats), indicating this baseline already exists in-market: https://campfiretechnology.com/faq/ and https://www.campfirewriting.com/plus

## Risks / trade-offs
- Significant engineering surface area (auth, encryption key UX, conflict UI, recovery testing).
- Key management friction can hurt activation if setup is not fast and clear.
- If restore/conflict UX is weak, perceived trust can drop below simpler competitor flows.

## Recommendation
Proceed, but keep the differentiation bar explicit in Design: make trust legible (status clarity, conflict explanations, safe restore history) and keep opt-in/key setup under 2 minutes.

## Handoff
Assign to: Design
